chunk_summary: This section discusses sodium and fluid restriction strategies in pediatric cirrhosis with ascites, emphasizing individualized sodium targets, cautious fluid restriction, and diuretic management to balance nutrition and renal function.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of sodium restriction in managing pediatric cirrhosis with ascites.
Identify when fluid restriction is appropriate based on serum sodium levels.
Learn practical diuretic dosing strategies for pediatric patients with cirrhosis.
Recognize the importance of maintaining calorie and protein intake during dietary restrictions.
key_facts: Sodium restriction is the primary dietary intervention in pediatric cirrhosis with ascites.
Fluid restriction is recommended only when serum sodium is ≤125 mmol/L and should be goal-directed.
Sodium intake should start at 1–2 mEq/kg/day and be adjusted based on age, ascites severity, and renal function.
Spironolactone is commonly used at 1–6 mg/kg/day, with furosemide added at similar doses if needed.
A spironolactone:furosemide ratio of 5:2 is a practical outpatient dosing strategy in Indian pediatric practice.
Monitor weight, serum sodium, potassium, and renal function regularly during diuretic therapy.
reference_quote: Major cirrhosis guidance supports fluid restriction only when there is moderate to severe hyponatremia.
document_key_topics: Sodium and fluid restriction in pediatric cirrhosis
